perturbation.original_text: Section 3. Confidentiality Obligation. Except as required by law, the Receiving Party shall treat as confidential and will not use (other than for the purpose of evaluating the Partner Application as stated in Section 2 above), disclose or otherwise make available any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to any person other than Representatives of the Receiving Party who have a business need to know Mutual Non-­‐Disclosure Agreement Version 2 2 for purposes of the Evaluation, and who have been advised by the Receiving Party of the terms and conditions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 3. Confidentiality Obligation. Except as required by law, the Receiving Party shall treat as confidential and will not use (other than for the purpose of evaluating the Partner Application as stated in Section 2 above), disclose or otherwise make available any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to any person other than Representatives of the Receiving Party who have a business need to know for purposes of the Evaluation, without advising them of the terms and conditions of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to advise representatives of the agreement's terms could lead to unintentional violations of confidentiality, conflicting with the legal duty to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the DTSA because failing to inform representatives of the confidentiality terms weakens the protection of trade secrets, potentially allowing for their misappropriation. The DTSA aims to safeguard trade secrets and requires companies to take reasonable measures to ensure their confidentiality.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the DTSA by removing the requirement for the Receiving Party to advise its representatives of the confidentiality terms. The Law Explanation states that the DTSA aims to safeguard trade secrets and requires companies to take reasonable measures to ensure their confidentiality. By removing the advisory requirement, the modified text weakens the protection of trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 18 U.S.C. 1836 which is part of the DTSA indicating this snippet is relevant, confirming the link between the DTSA and the protection of trade secrets. The original text ensured representatives were aware of the agreement terms, whereas the modified text does not, potentially leading to misappropriation as it weakens protective measures.

perturbation.original_text: Section 13. Term. This Agreement shall remain in effect for three years from the Effective Date.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 13. Term. This Agreement shall remain in effect indefinitely from the Effective Date.
perturbation.explanation: An indefinite term for a non-disclosure agreement can be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under New York law, especially if it lacks specific limitations or triggers for termination.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York law regarding contract duration and reasonableness
perturbation.law_citation: NCRIC, Inc. v. Computer Care Servs., Inc., 2024 N.Y. Slip Op. 01805
perturbation.law_explanation: Under New York law, non-disclosure agreements with unreasonably long or indefinite terms can be deemed unenforceable. The shift from a defined 3-year term to an indefinite one increases the likelihood of the agreement being challenged and invalidated, as it lacks a clear endpoint or termination event, potentially violating legal principles of contract reasonableness.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a three-year term for the agreement, while the changed text makes the term indefinite. The Law Explanation states that under New York law, non-disclosure agreements with unreasonably long or indefinite terms can be deemed unenforceable. The Perturbation Explanation similarly indicates that an indefinite term for a non-disclosure agreement can be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under New York law. Thus, by changing the term from a defined period to indefinite, the contract contradicts the legal principle that such agreements should have reasonable limitations.

perturbation.original_text: Section 7. Notice of Unauthorized Use. The Receiving Party will notify the Disclosing Party promptly upon discovery of any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information or any other breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party, and will reasonably cooperate with the Disclosing Party to regain possession of the Confidential Information and prevent its further unauthorized use or disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 7. Notice of Unauthorized Use. The Receiving Party will notify the Disclosing Party of any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information or any other breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party within 90 days of discovery, and will cooperate with the Disclosing Party to regain possession of the Confidential Information and prevent its further unauthorized use or disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the notification period to 90 days could hinder the Disclosing Party's ability to promptly mitigate damages and pursue legal remedies, conflicting with the duty to protect trade secrets diligently.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the DTSA because delaying notification of unauthorized use or disclosure of confidential information to 90 days is not prompt and does not allow enough time to take actions to minimize loss. Under the DTSA, companies must take reasonable and prompt measures to protect their trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the notification timeframe for unauthorized use of confidential information from 'promptly' to 'within 90 days.' The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state this delay could hinder the Disclosing Party's ability to mitigate damages and that the DTSA requires 'reasonable and prompt measures' to protect trade secrets. The scraped snippet references the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016. Delaying notification to 90 days does not align with the legal expectation of prompt action to protect trade secrets, creating a contradiction as it weakens the Disclosing Party's ability to respond effectively, impacting their legal rights.

perturbation.original_text: Section 11. Notices. All notices, requests, consents, demands and other communications provided for by this Agreement will be in writing and shall be deemed sufficient if delivered in person or by express courier to the party to be notified.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 11. Notices. All notices, requests, consents, demands and other communications provided for by this Agreement may be delivered verbally and shall be deemed sufficient upon utterance to the party to be notified.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring only verbal notice is not sufficient for legal contracts. It conflicts with the Statute of Frauds, which requires certain contracts to be in writing to be enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the New York Statute of Frauds (NY CLS Gen Oblig § 5-701), which requires that certain agreements, particularly those not performable within one year, must be in writing to be enforceable. By allowing verbal notices, the contract fails to meet the statutory requirement of a written agreement, making it potentially unenforceable.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability up TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability next SECTION 5-702 Requirements for use of plain language in consumer transactions This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-701 Agreements required to be in writing General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 7 § 5-701. Agreements required to be in writing. a. Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith, or by his lawful agent, if such agreement, promise or undertaking: 1. By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof or the performance of which is not to be completed before the end of a lifetime; 2. Is a special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person; 3. Is made in consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; 5. Is a subsequent or new promise to pay a debt discharged in bankruptcy; 6. Notwithstanding section 2-201 of the uniform commercial code, if the goods be sold at public auction, and the auctioneer at the time of the sale, enters in a sale book, a memorandum specifying the nature and price of the property sold, the terms of the sale, the name of the purchaser, and the name of the person on whose account the sale was made, such memorandum is equivalent in effect to a note of the contract or sale, subscribed by the party to be charged therewith; 9. Is a contract to assign or an assignment, with or without consideration to the promisor, of a life or health or accident insurance policy, or a promise, with or without consideration to the promisor, to name a beneficiary of any such policy. This provision shall not apply to a policy of industrial life or health or accident insurance. 10. Is a contract to pay compensation for services rendered in negotiating a loan, or in negotiating the purchase, sale, exchange, renting or leasing of any real estate or interest therein, or of a business opportunity, business, its good will, inventory, fixtures or an interest therein, including a majority of the voting stock interest in a corporation and including the creating of a partnership interest.
perturbation.justification: The changed text, by allowing verbal notices, directly contradicts the New York Statute of Frauds (NY CLS Gen Oblig § 5-701), which requires certain agreements to be in writing to be enforceable. The scraped law snippet 2 explicitly states, 'Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith.' This clearly indicates that verbal agreements are not sufficient for agreements covered under the statute of frauds. The original text required written notice, aligning with the Statute of Frauds, while the changed text allows verbal notice, leading to a clear non-compliance with the law.
